primary_diagnosis.name: Iron-overload disorder (hereditary haemochromatosis) presenting with new-onset atrial fibrillation and hepatic involvement

comorbidities.name: New-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Early congestive cardiac failure (elevated JVP, exertional dyspnoea)
Recently diagnosed type 2 diabetes mellitus (secondary / 'bronze diabetes')

pathophysiology_summary: Progressive parenchymal iron loading (in hereditary haemochromatosis, most commonly HFE-related) deposits excess iron in the liver, heart, pancreas, skin, joints and pituitary/gonadal axis. Over years this produces the classic multi-system picture seen here: hepatic infiltration (hepatomegaly, evolving cirrhosis, RUQ ache), pancreatic islet damage (secondary 'bronze' diabetes), dermal iron plus melanin (slate-grey/bronze skin), a characteristic 2nd/3rd MCP arthropathy, and hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism (reduced libido). The acute decompensation is cardiac: iron cardiomyopathy and atrial infiltration precipitate new atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response and early heart failure. AF confers a substantial thromboembolic-stroke risk and, with underlying cardiomyopathy, a risk of haemodynamic deterioration. Definitive diagnosis requires iron studies (elevated transferrin saturation and ferritin), LFTs, HFE genotyping and often imaging — none obtainable via telehealth. Definitive treatment is iron removal by therapeutic venesection/phlebotomy; the acute issue needs rate control, an anticoagulation decision and cardiac assessment in hospital.

differential.diagnosis: new-onset atrial fibrillation (cause to be determined)
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden sustained fast, irregular palpitations with exertional breathlessness in an unwell adult.
RUQ pain — but the dull constant character and systemic features point away from simple biliary colic.
Bronze skin + new diabetes + chronic MCP arthralgia + hepatomegaly together suggest one unifying systemic disease rather than coincidence.

differential.diagnosis: iron-overload disorder (haemochromatosis) with cardiac and hepatic complications
differential.evidence_basis: The constellation — bronze pigmentation, secondary diabetes, 2nd/3rd MCP arthropathy, hepatomegaly, hypogonadism, now new AF — is the classic iron-overload phenotype.
No acute jaundice or fever; the bronzing and chronicity of arthralgia argue against an acute hepatitic illness.
2nd/3rd MCP involvement is atypical for wear-and-tear osteoarthritis and is a hallmark of iron-deposition arthropathy.

differential.diagnosis: iron-overload disorder (haemochromatosis) with new AF, early heart failure and hepatic involvement
differential.evidence_basis: Unifying systemic diagnosis with an acute, time-critical cardiac complication; confirmation needs iron studies, LFTs, HFE genotyping and cardiac workup that only hospital assessment can provide.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm the underlying diagnosis or the arrhythmia. The AI Doctor is scored on (a) recognising a likely new sustained tachyarrhythmia with heart-failure/stroke risk, (b) suspecting a unifying systemic disorder behind the multi-organ picture, and (c) arranging urgent emergency transfer — NOT on naming or confirming the diagnosis.

red_flags.description: New-onset sustained rapid irregular heartbeat with exertional dyspnoea — likely new atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and early heart failure
red_flags.clinical_significance: New AF carries a high thromboembolic-stroke risk and, with underlying iron cardiomyopathy, a risk of haemodynamic deterioration; needs urgent ECG, rate control and an anticoagulation decision.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated rapid AF can progress to haemodynamic collapse or cause an embolic stroke.

red_flags.description: No rate-controlling or anticoagulant medication in a patient with a new tachyarrhythmia
red_flags.clinical_significance: The arrhythmia is untreated and unanticoagulated, so both the rate and the stroke risk are currently unmitigated.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay in initiating rate control and stroke-prevention increases the window for embolic and haemodynamic events.

red_flags.description: Firm hepatomegaly with RUQ pain suggesting hepatic infiltration / evolving cirrhosis (clinician-only finding)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals advanced organ involvement from iron overload; contributes to overall acuity but is not something the patient can self-detect.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciating end-organ damage may lead to under-triage of overall severity.

red_flags.description: Multi-organ systemic constellation (bronze skin + secondary diabetes + MCP arthropathy + hepatomegaly + hypogonadism) pointing to a single infiltrative disorder
red_flags.clinical_significance: Recognising the unifying pattern is the diagnostic key; treating each symptom separately misses the disease.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Premature closure on the arrhythmia alone delays recognition of a treatable systemic iron-overload disorder.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the palpitations as an isolated arrhythmia and ignoring the bronze skin, new diabetes, arthralgia and hepatomegaly that point to a unifying systemic disease.
Attributing the RUQ ache to gallstones/biliary colic — the pain is dull and constant, not colicky, and sits within a systemic picture.
Dismissing the 2nd/3rd MCP arthralgia as ordinary osteoarthritis, missing the characteristic iron-deposition arthropathy.
Attempting to confirm the diagnosis or manage the arrhythmia remotely, or falsely reassuring, instead of arranging urgent emergency assessment for an ECG-diagnosed, unanticoagulated new AF.
Offering a rate-control (e.g. beta-blocker) or anticoagulant dose, or altering the diabetes medication, without an ECG, bloods and a prescribing clinician.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Whether the pulse is truly irregularly irregular / the presence of a pulse deficit
Heart rate, blood pressure and rhythm without a connected device
Elevated jugular venous pressure and other heart-failure signs on examination
Firm hepatomegaly extending below the costal margin on palpation
Transferrin saturation, serum ferritin, LFTs and HFE genotype
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a likely new sustained tachyarrhythmia with heart-failure and stroke risk in a systemically-unwell patient, keep the patient at rest, and arrange urgent emergency transfer to an emergency department by ambulance (the patient is bedbound and unsafe to self-transport); escalate to 000 immediately if chest pain, severe breathlessness, fainting or collapse develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Naming or confirming haemochromatosis or the arrhythmia remotely is not possible; the real risk is UNDER-action — reassuring, deferring to clinic hours, or trying to manage the arrhythmia at home instead of escalating.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NHFA-CSANZ Australian Clinical Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation 2018
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: New-onset AF with a rapid ventricular response and features of haemodynamic compromise or heart failure warrants urgent assessment; stroke-risk and rate/rhythm control are decided with ECG and clinical review.

Diagnosis rests on transferrin saturation and ferritin with HFE genotyping; definitive treatment is iron depletion by therapeutic venesection. Alcohol and vitamin C (which increases iron absorption) should be avoided.
